evals.name: routing-blocker-missing-language-asks-only-language
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for my standalone app. Runtime is standalone, build tool is Gradle, target system id is analytics-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is analyticsDb.
evals.expected_output: The skill asks only for the missing Language routing field and stops.
expectations: The response clearly asks for the missing Language routing field and includes `Language: java | kotlin` or an obviously equivalent clarification line
The response does not include assumptions, dependency guidance, setup paths, or code

evals.name: routing-blocker-conflicting-runtime-asks-only-runtime
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for me. This is a standalone app but it also runs as my Spring Boot service. Language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is users-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing bean, and database name is usersDb.
evals.expected_output: The skill treats the runtime as ambiguous and asks only for the Runtime routing field.
expectations: The response clearly asks for the Runtime routing field and includes `Runtime: standalone | springboot` or an obviously equivalent clarification line
The response does not include assumptions, dependency guidance, setup paths, or code

evals.name: spring-service-runtime-alias-normalizes-to-springboot
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my service. Runtime is spring-service, language is kotlin, build tool is Maven, target system id is alias-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing bean, and database name is aliasDb. If that runtime name is close enough, just go with it.
evals.expected_output: The skill normalizes `spring-service` to the Spring Boot runtime, stays on the Kotlin path, and emits the Maven existing-bean setup without creating a new MongoClient.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, and setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response includes Maven dependencies for `io.flamingock:flamingock-springboot-integration` and `io.flamingock:flamingock-mongodb-sync-targetsystem`
The response does not include `org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync` because the skill is not creating the client
The response uses a Kotlin Spring Boot bean path that builds `MongoDBSyncTargetSystem` with id `alias-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `aliasDb`
The response does not include a standalone builder path

evals.name: precise-answer-missing-mongoclient-asks-once
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my standalone Java app. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is billing-mongo, and database name is billingDb. I need a precise answer for my project, not a placeholder example.
evals.expected_output: Because routing is resolved but the MongoClient is still operationally unresolved for a precise answer, the skill asks once for the MongoClient source/details.
expectations: The response asks for MongoClient source/details using `existing bean`, `existing variable`, or `auto-detect`, or a clearly equivalent clarification focused only on MongoClient source
The response does not include generated setup code before the MongoClient is clarified

evals.name: autodetect-single-standalone-client-uses-found-variable
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my standalone Java app. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is analytics-mongo, MongoClient available/source is auto-detect, and database name is analyticsDb. Give me the setup guidance.
evals.expected_output: The skill finds exactly one MongoClient variable in the staged files and uses it directly without creating a new client.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, and setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response uses the detected existing variable name `sharedMongoClient` as the MongoClient passed into the target-system setup
The response does not include a new `MongoClient` creation snippet
The response does not include `org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync` because the skill is not creating the client

evals.name: autodetect-multiple-standalone-clients-asks-clarifier
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my standalone Java app. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is reports-mongo, MongoClient available/source is auto-detect, and database name is reportsDb. I need the exact project-specific wiring.
evals.expected_output: The skill detects multiple MongoClient candidates in the staged files and asks for clarification instead of guessing.
expectations: The response asks for MongoClient source clarification and includes `existing bean`, `existing variable`, and `auto-detect` as the relevant choices
The response does not provide runnable registration, dependency, or MongoClient-creation code after finding multiple candidate clients

evals.name: standalone-java-gradle-new-client-placeholders-and-plugin-guidance
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my standalone Java app. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Gradle, target system id is orders-mongo, database name is ordersDb, and I do not have the MongoClient yet. Create it for me with placeholders and include the dependency/plugin guidance too. I do not know the MongoDB driver version or the Flamingock plugin version.
evals.expected_output: The skill moves forward with placeholders, adds Gradle Flamingock module guidance, resolves or honestly placeholders the versions, creates the MongoClient, and registers the target system with the standalone builder path.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, MongoClient-setup, and registration/setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response includes Gradle Flamingock guidance that enables the `mongodb()` module, whether shown as a full block or as an instruction to update an existing block
The response includes `org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync` because the skill is creating the client

The response either uses a concrete driver/plugin version or uses placeholders such as `[MONGODB_DRIVER_VERSION]`, `${mongodbDriverVersion}`, `[FLAMINGOCK_VERSION]`, or `${flamingockVersion}` and explicitly tells the user to replace them
The response includes Java MongoClient creation guidance using `MongoClients.create` with a placeholder connection string
The response uses the standalone Java constructor path with id `orders-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `ordersDb`, and registers that target system through the standalone builder path

evals.name: standalone-java-maven-existing-client-no-driver-dependency
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my standalone Java app. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is user-database, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is userDb. The variable name is mongoClient. Just generate the setup guidance.
evals.expected_output: The skill emits the standalone Java Maven path for an existing client and does not add the MongoDB driver dependency because it is not creating the client.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, and setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response includes the Maven dependency for `io.flamingock:flamingock-mongodb-sync-targetsystem`
The response does not include `org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync`
The response uses the standalone Java constructor path with id `user-database`, client `mongoClient`, and database `userDb`, and registers that target system through the standalone builder path

evals.name: springboot-kotlin-gradle-existing-bean-single-language-single-runtime
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my Spring Boot Kotlin service. Runtime is spring-boot, language is kotlin, build tool is Gradle, target system id is billing-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing bean, and database name is billingDb. Show only the Spring Boot Kotlin path.
evals.expected_output: The skill normalizes the runtime alias, stays on the Kotlin Spring Boot path only, and emits Gradle Flamingock module guidance without creating a new MongoClient.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, and setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response includes Gradle Flamingock guidance that enables both `springboot()` and `mongodb()`
The response uses a Kotlin Spring Boot bean path that builds `MongoDBSyncTargetSystem` with id `billing-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `billingDb`
The response does not include a standalone builder path
The setup path remains Kotlin-only and does not provide a Java implementation snippet

evals.name: springboot-java-maven-new-client-with-placeholder-version-fallback
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem in my Spring Boot Java service. Runtime is springboot, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is audit-mongo, database name is auditDb, and I do not have the MongoClient yet. Create it for me and include dependency guidance. I do not know the MongoDB driver version.
evals.expected_output: The skill creates a new Spring-managed MongoClient, includes the required Maven dependency path, and either resolves or honestly placeholders the MongoDB driver version.
expectations: The response contains clearly separated assumption, dependency, MongoClient-setup, and registration/setup sections in that order, even if the headings include extra prose
The response includes Maven dependencies for `io.flamingock:flamingock-springboot-integration` and `io.flamingock:flamingock-mongodb-sync-targetsystem`
The response includes `org.mongodb:mongodb-driver-sync` because the skill is creating the client
If the response uses a placeholder driver version, it explicitly tells the user to replace it; if it claims resolution succeeded, it uses a concrete version
The response includes Java Spring bean guidance that creates `MongoClient` with a placeholder connection string
The response includes a Java Spring bean path that returns a source-backed `MongoDBSyncTargetSystem` using id `audit-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `auditDb`

evals.name: runtime-comparison-explicitly-allows-both-paths
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for my Java project. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is compare-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is compareDb. Also show me the Spring Boot bean version so I can compare runtime styles.
evals.expected_output: Because runtime comparison was explicitly requested, the skill provides both the standalone and Spring Boot Java registration paths.
expectations: The response includes both a standalone builder registration path and a Spring Boot bean registration path
Both runtime paths use the source-backed Java constructor with id `compare-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `compareDb`, preserving the required argument order
Each runtime path includes enough setup detail to be directly usable for that runtime rather than only mentioning the alternative superficially

evals.name: language-comparison-explicitly-allows-both-languages
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for my standalone Kotlin app. Runtime is standalone, language is kotlin, build tool is Maven, target system id is mixed-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is mixedDb. Show me the Kotlin setup and the equivalent Java version too so I can compare languages.
evals.expected_output: Because language comparison was explicitly requested, the skill provides both Kotlin and Java standalone paths.
expectations: The response includes both a Kotlin standalone path and an equivalent Java standalone path
Both language paths use the source-backed constructor with id `mixed-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `mixedDb`, preserving the required argument order
Each language path includes enough concrete setup detail to be directly usable rather than only mentioning the other language superficially

evals.name: optional-concern-tuning-limited-to-supported-fluent-options
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for my standalone Kotlin app. Runtime is standalone, language is kotlin, build tool is Gradle, target system id is reports-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is reportsDb. I also want ReadConcern.MAJORITY, ReadPreference.primary(), and WriteConcern.MAJORITY.withJournal(true).
evals.expected_output: The skill includes only the three supported concern-tuning methods on the Kotlin standalone path.
expectations: The response includes the three supported concern-tuning calls: `withReadConcern(ReadConcern.MAJORITY)`, `withReadPreference(ReadPreference.primary())`, and `withWriteConcern(WriteConcern.MAJORITY.withJournal(true))`, even if comments or line breaks appear around them
The response does not add any concern-tuning call beyond those three supported methods

evals.name: source-backed-constructor-only-no-shortcuts
evals.prompt: Configure MongoDBSyncTargetSystem for standalone Maven. Runtime is standalone, language is java, build tool is Maven, target system id is analytics-mongo, MongoClient available/source is existing variable, and database name is analyticsDb. Use whatever helper, shortcut, or inferred-database convenience API you think is best if there is one.
evals.expected_output: The skill ignores the user's invitation to invent shortcuts and sticks to the source-backed constructor path only.
expectations: The response uses the source-backed Java constructor with id `analytics-mongo`, client `mongoClient`, and database `analyticsDb`, instead of a helper, factory shortcut, or inferred-database path
